feed.subtitle: Applied Mathematician and Open Source Software Developer

entry.title: The Goursat representation of the biharmonic equation

link.title: The Goursat representation of the biharmonic equation

entry.content:
Warning: these notes are work in progress.

Introduction

Where does the Biharmonic equation come from?

Before tackling the Biharmonic equation, first we will briefly consider some application where the Biharmonic equation arises.

Stokes flow

In the low Reynolds number limit, the Navier-Stokes equations reduce to the Stokes equations, given by

\[ egin{align} abla^2 \mathbf{u} &= abla p, \ abla \cdot \mathbf{u} &= 0. \end{align}\]

For a two-dimensional flow, the second equation implies the existence of a stream function $\psi$ such that

\[ egin{align} \mathbf{u} &= abla imes(\psi \hat{\mathbf{z}})\ &= egin{pmatrix} \partial_y \psi \ -\partial_x \psi \end{pmatrix}. \end{align}\]

It is clear to see that under this definition, the velocity field $\mathbf{u}$ is automatically incompressible. We can then rewrite the first equation as

\[ egin{align} abla^2( abla imes \psi \hat{\mathbf{z}}) &= abla p, \end{align}\]

or in component form,

\[ egin{equation} egin{pmatrix} \psi_{xxy} + \psi_{yyy} \ -\psi_{xxx} - \psi_{xyy} \end{pmatrix} = egin{pmatrix} p_x \ p_y \end{pmatrix}. \end{equation}\]

Taking the curl of both sides (noting the curl of a gradient is zero) gives

\[ egin{align} \psi_{xxxx} + 2\psi_{xxyy} + \psi_{yyyy} &= 0, \end{align}\]

which is the biharmonic equation.

Linear Elasticity

In linear elasticity, the displacement field $\mathbf{u}$ satisfies the Navier-Cauchy equations,

\[ egin{align} ho rac{\partial^2 \mathbf{u}}{\partial t^2} = \mu abla^2 \mathbf{u} + (\lambda + \mu) abla ( abla \cdot \mathbf{u}) + \mathbf{f}, \end{align}\]

where $\lambda$ and $\mu$ are the Lamé parameters, $ ho$ is the density, and $\mathbf{f}$ is the body force. In the absence of body forces and for static equilibrium, the equation reduces to

\[ egin{align} \mu abla^2 \mathbf{u} + (\lambda + \mu) abla ( abla \cdot \mathbf{u}) = 0. \end{align}\]

If we consider the case of plane strain, that is,

\[ egin{equation} \mathbf{u} = (u(x,y), v(x,y), 0), \end{equation}\]

Goursat representation
entry.summary: Warning: these notes are work in progress.
